When you type node app.js into your terminal, a complex symphony of C++, C, and JavaScript fires up. For years, the Node.js community has relied on a simplified explanation of how it works: "It's single-threaded and uses an event loop."
That explanation is a lie by omission.
If Node.js were truly single-threaded, it would be incapable of handling the massive I/O workloads it’s famous for. It would crumble under the weight of a single file read operation.
To truly understand Node.js, we have to strip away the JavaScript layer and look at the engine room. We need to look at V8, libuv, C++ bindings, and the operating system kernel.
Let’s dive deep into the internals of the Node.js runtime.
The Holy Trinity: V8, libuv, and Node Core
Press enter or click to view image in full size

At its core, Node.js is not a framework; it is a C++ application. If you look at the Node.js source code, you’ll find a massive C++ codebase that bridges two entirely distinct technologies:

• V8 (The JavaScript Engine): Google’s C++ engine that parses, interprets, and JIT-compiles JavaScript into machine code. V8 provides the heap (memory management) and the execution context.

• libuv (The I/O Engine): A C library originally written…
